privacyPolicy.title: Política de Privacidad
privacyPolicy.subtitle: Tu privacidad es importante para nosotros. Este aviso explica cómo manejamos tu información.
privacyPolicy.footerNote: Al continuar usando Pregnancy Toolkits, reconoces que has leído y comprendido esta Política de Privacidad.

privacyFirst.title: Enfoque de Privacidad Primero
privacyFirst.content: Ofrecemos todos nuestros servicios sin recopilar datos personales. Nuestra aplicación está especializada en proporcionar herramientas de apoyo a mujeres embarazadas. Todos los datos de salud se almacenan localmente en tu dispositivo.

collection.title: Información que recopilamos
collection.desc: Recopilamos información personal que nos proporcionas voluntariamente cuando expresas interés en nuestros productos y servicios.
collection.paymentSecure: Los datos de pago son manejados de forma segura por Google Play

dataSafety.title: Seguridad de datos
dataSafety.desc: Sus datos están protegidos de forma segura

localStorage.title: Almacenamiento local de datos
localStorage.desc: Todos los datos de salud personales, citas e información de seguimiento se almacenan únicamente de forma local en tu dispositivo.
localStorage.neverLeaves: Los datos nunca salen de tu dispositivo
localStorage.notUploaded: No se suben a nuestros servidores

processing.title: Cómo procesamos la información
processing.desc: Procesamos tu información para proporcionar, mejorar y administrar nuestros servicios, comunicarnos contigo, para seguridad y prevención de fraude.
processing.legalBasis: Solo procesamos tu información cuando tenemos una razón legal válida para hacerlo.

advertising.title: Publicidad (Google AdSense)
advertising.desc: Esta aplicación muestra anuncios a través de Google AdSense para permitir el acceso gratuito a todas las herramientas. Google puede recopilar y utilizar ciertos datos para mostrar anuncios relevantes.
advertising.googleCollects: Los anuncios son proporcionados por Google AdSense — Google gestiona todo el procesamiento de datos publicitarios
advertising.adId: Google puede recopilar el identificador publicitario de tu dispositivo (GAID/IDFA) para personalizar los anuncios
advertising.noPersonalSent: NO enviamos ninguno de tus datos de salud personales a Google ni a ningún anunciante
advertising.optOut: Puedes desactivar los anuncios personalizados en la configuración de tu dispositivo (Ajustes → Google → Anuncios)
advertising.consentBased: La personalización de anuncios sigue las preferencias de consentimiento de tu dispositivo
advertising.policyLink: Para más detalles sobre cómo Google usa tus datos, consulta:

sharing.title: Compartir información
sharing.desc: Podemos compartir información en situaciones específicas con terceros específicos:
sharing.businessTransfers: Transferencias comerciales: durante fusiones, ventas o adquisiciones
sharing.noSold: NO hemos vendido ni compartido información personal con terceros con fines comerciales en los últimos 12 meses.

aiProducts.title: Funciones impulsadas por IA
aiProducts.desc: Ofrecemos funciones impulsadas por inteligencia artificial a través de modelos IA seguros en la nube (Google Gemini). Ningún proveedor externo tiene acceso a tus datos de salud almacenados.
aiProducts.dataNote: Al usar funciones de IA, solo el texto de tu consulta se envía de forma anónima a nuestro servidor seguro. Tus datos de salud permanecen completamente en tu dispositivo.
aiProducts.noTraining: Tus consultas NO se utilizan para entrenar modelos de IA

notifications.title: Sonidos de notificación
notifications.desc: Nuestra aplicación utiliza sonidos de notificación generados localmente mediante la API Web Audio.
notifications.noAudioCollected: No se recopilan ni transmiten datos de audio

retention.title: Retención de datos
retention.desc: Conservamos tu información el tiempo necesario para cumplir con los propósitos descritos en este aviso de privacidad. Cuando no tengamos necesidad comercial legítima, eliminaremos o anonimizaremos dicha información.

security.title: Seguridad de la información
security.desc: Hemos implementado medidas de seguridad técnicas y organizativas apropiadas para proteger la seguridad de cualquier información personal que procesamos.
security.note: Sin embargo, ninguna transmisión electrónica por Internet puede garantizarse como 100% segura.

minors.title: Información de menores
minors.desc: No recopilamos datos intencionalmente de menores de 18 años. Al usar los servicios, declaras que tienes al menos 18 años.

rights.title: Tus derechos de privacidad
rights.desc: Según tu ubicación, puedes tener ciertos derechos sobre tu información personal:
rights.access: Derecho a acceder a tus datos personales
rights.optOut: Derecho a rechazar el procesamiento de datos

international.title: Usuarios internacionales
international.desc: Cumplimos con las leyes de protección de datos aplicables, incluido el RGPD (UE/UK) y leyes regionales de privacidad.
international.eu: UE/UK: Cumplimiento del RGPD con procesamiento basado en consentimiento
international.us: EE.UU.: Derechos de privacidad específicos por estado (California, Virginia, etc.)
international.canada: Canadá: Marco de consentimiento expreso e implícito

acceptance.content: Al usar Herramientas de Embarazo, aceptas estar sujeta a estos términos y condiciones. Si no estás de acuerdo con alguna parte de estos términos, por favor no uses nuestra aplicación.

medical.title: Aviso educativo
medical.warning: ⚠️ Muy importante: por favor lee cuidadosamente
medical.content: Herramientas de Embarazo está destinada únicamente con fines informativos y educativos. Esta aplicación:
medical.noAdvice: NO proporciona consejos médicos, diagnósticos o tratamientos
medical.noReplace: NO reemplaza la consulta con un médico o profesional de salud
medical.noEmergency: NO debe usarse para decisiones médicas de emergencia
medical.consultDoctor: Siempre consulta a tu médico o profesional de salud calificado sobre cualquier pregunta médica o de salud.

usage.title: Uso de la aplicación
usage.content: Aceptas lo siguiente al usar nuestra aplicación:
usage.personal: Usar la app solo para fines personales y no comerciales
usage.notSubstitute: No depender de los resultados de la app como sustituto de atención médica
usage.seekCare: Buscar atención médica inmediata en situaciones de emergencia
usage.noMisuse: Sin mal uso de los servicios

intellectualProperty.content: Todo el contenido está protegido por derechos de autor.

userContent.title: Contenido del usuario
userContent.content: Usted conserva los derechos sobre su contenido.

accuracy.title: Exactitud de la información
accuracy.content: Aunque nos esforzamos por proporcionar información precisa y actualizada, no garantizamos la exactitud o integridad de la información en la app. Los cálculos y estimaciones son aproximados y pueden diferir de la realidad. Siempre verifica la información importante con tu profesional de salud.

appStore.content: Se aplican los términos adicionales de la tienda.

liability.title: Limitación de responsabilidad
liability.content: No seremos responsables de ningún daño resultante del uso o la imposibilidad de usar la app, incluidas las decisiones de salud tomadas con base en la información proporcionada. Usas esta aplicación bajo tu propio riesgo.

modifications.title: Modificaciones de los términos
modifications.content: Nos reservamos el derecho de modificar estos términos en cualquier momento sin previo aviso. Tu uso continuado de la app después de cualquier cambio constituye tu aceptación de los términos modificados. Te animamos a revisar estos términos periódicamente.

symptomAnalyzer.title: Diario de bienestar del embarazo
symptomAnalyzer.subtitle: Registra y reflexiona sobre tu bienestar diario
symptomAnalyzer.disclaimer: Esta herramienta es solo para diario y fines educativos. Consulta siempre a tu médico.
symptomAnalyzer.discussWithProvider: Comparte tu diario con tu médico en tu próxima visita
symptomAnalyzer.remember: Cada embarazo es único. Este diario te ayuda a reflexionar sobre tu viaje y compartir notas con tu consultor.

nausea.early: Muy común en el primer trimestre. Muchas mujeres encuentran útiles las comidas pequeñas y frecuentes y el jengibre.
nausea.late: Menos común en esta etapa. Si persiste, menciónalo a tu consultor en tu próxima visita.

fatigue.early: Tu cuerpo está haciendo un trabajo increíble ahora. Prioriza el descanso cuando puedas.
fatigue.mid: Muchas mujeres se sienten más energizadas en el segundo trimestre — pero escucha a tu cuerpo.

headache.general: Mantenerse hidratada y descansar en un ambiente tranquilo puede ayudar. Anota la frecuencia para tu consultor.

backpain.early: Anota cuándo ocurre. Los estiramientos suaves pueden proporcionar alivio.
backpain.late: Común a medida que tu cuerpo se adapta. Buena postura y movimiento suave pueden ayudar.

cramps.general: Las sensaciones leves pueden ser normales durante el embarazo. Anota el momento y la intensidad en tu registro.

swelling.general: La hinchazón leve en pies y tobillos es común, especialmente más adelante. Elevar los pies y mantenerse hidratada puede ayudar.

heartburn.general: Muy común durante el embarazo. Comidas más pequeñas y evitar acostarse justo después de comer puede ayudar.

insomnia.general: Las dificultades para dormir son comunes. Una rutina de sueño constante y una posición cómoda pueden ayudar.

moodswings.general: Los cambios emocionales son normales durante el embarazo. Conectar con tu red de apoyo puede ser reconfortante.

dizziness.general: Levantarse despacio, mantenerse hidratada y comer regularmente puede ayudar. Anota la frecuencia para tu consultor.

insights.default: Esta es una experiencia común durante el embarazo. Registrarla te ayuda a seguir patrones.
insights.defaultFallback: Anotado en tu diario. Comparte tu diario con tu consultor en tu próxima visita.

fitnessCoach.title: Guía Fitness IA
fitnessCoach.subtitle: Ejercicios seguros personalizados para tu etapa de embarazo
fitnessCoach.disclaimer: Consulta a tu médico antes de comenzar cualquier programa de ejercicios.
fitnessCoach.fitnessVideosSubtitle: Guías de entrenamiento seguro para cada trimestre
fitnessCoach.safetyText: Para inmediatamente si sientes mareos, dolor o dificultad para respirar. Mantén agua cerca.

exerciseDescs.prenatalSquatsDesc: Párate con los pies a la anchura de los hombros. Baja suavemente como si te sentaras.
exerciseDescs.birdDogDesc: En cuatro puntos, extiende el brazo y la pierna opuestos. Mantén el equilibrio.
exerciseDescs.pelvicTiltsDesc: Balancea suavemente tu pelvis hacia adelante y atrás para aliviar la espalda baja.
exerciseDescs.wallPushupsDesc: Párate a un brazo de distancia de la pared. Inclínate y empuja hacia atrás.
exerciseDescs.butterflyStretchDesc: Siéntate con los pies juntos, rodillas hacia afuera. Inclínate suavemente hacia adelante.
exerciseDescs.seatedMarchingDesc: Siéntate en una silla estable o pelota. Levanta las rodillas alternadamente.
exerciseDescs.neckRollsDesc: Gira la cabeza lentamente en círculos suaves. Cambia de dirección después de 15 segundos.
exerciseDescs.armCirclesDesc: Extiende los brazos a los lados y haz pequeños círculos. Aumenta gradualmente el tamaño.
exerciseDescs.gluteBridgeDesc: Acuéstate boca arriba con las rodillas flexionadas. Levanta las caderas hacia el techo y mantén.
exerciseDescs.sideLyingLegDesc: Acuéstate de lado. Levanta la pierna superior lentamente y bájala con control.
exerciseDescs.stepTouchDesc: Da pasos laterales a un ritmo controlado. Mantén el core activado.
exerciseDescs.catCowDesc: En cuatro puntos, alterna entre arquear y redondear la espalda.
exerciseDescs.sideStretchDesc: De pie, extiende un brazo sobre la cabeza e inclínate suavemente hacia un lado.
exerciseDescs.childPoseDesc: Arrodíllate y siéntate sobre los talones. Extiende los brazos hacia adelante.
exerciseDescs.deepBreathingDesc: Inhala por la nariz contando 4, mantén 4, exhala por la boca contando 6.
exerciseDescs.ankleCirclesDesc: Lift one foot and rotate your ankle in slow circles, then switch direction.
exerciseDescs.hipCirclesDesc: Stand with feet apart and rotate your hips in wide circles.
exerciseDescs.shoulderShrugsDesc: Lift your shoulders towards your ears, then slowly lower them. Repeat.
exerciseDescs.wallSitDesc: Lean your back against a wall and slide down until knees are at 90 degrees. Hold.
exerciseDescs.calfRaisesDesc: Stand straight, raise your heels off the ground, hold, then lower slowly.
exerciseDescs.sumoSquatDesc: Stand with feet wider than shoulders, toes pointed out, and lower down.
exerciseDescs.standingMarchDesc: March in place, lifting knees high alternately while swinging arms.
exerciseDescs.lowImpactJacksDesc: Open arms and step one leg out at a time — a gentle version of jumping jacks.
exerciseDescs.kegelExerciseDesc: Squeeze your pelvic floor muscles as if stopping urination. Hold 5 seconds, release.
exerciseDescs.seatedTwistDesc: Sit upright and slowly twist your torso to the right, then left, while breathing.
exerciseDescs.hamstringStretchDesc: Sit and extend one leg forward, gently reaching for your toes.
exerciseDescs.figureFourDesc: Lie down, place one ankle on the opposite knee, and pull gently towards you.
exerciseDescs.savasanaDesc: Lie on your back or side, close your eyes, breathe deeply and relax completely.
exerciseDescs.gentleTwistDesc: Lie on your back, drop both knees gently to one side, then switch.

t1.title: Primer Trimestre (Semanas 1-13)
t1.text: Concéntrate en ejercicios de baja intensidad. Evita el sobrecalentamiento y mantente hidratada.

t2.title: Segundo Trimestre (Semanas 14-26)
t2.text: ¡Tu energía es más alta! Buen momento para ejercicio moderado. Evita estar acostada boca arriba mucho tiempo.

t3.title: Tercer Trimestre (Semanas 27-42)
t3.text: Enfócate en estiramientos suaves y ejercicios de respiración. Evita movimientos de alto impacto.

warmup.title: Rutina de Calentamiento
warmup.step1: Comienza con rotaciones suaves de cuello y encogimiento de hombros.
warmup.step2: Haz círculos de brazos, empezando pequeños y aumentando gradualmente.
warmup.step3: Marcha suavemente en el lugar durante 30 segundos.
warmup.step4: Toma 5 respiraciones profundas expandiendo completamente las costillas.

cooldown.title: Rutina de Enfriamiento
cooldown.step1: Camina lentamente en el lugar 30 segundos para bajar el ritmo cardíaco.
cooldown.step2: Estira suavemente el cuello y los hombros, manteniendo cada uno 15 segundos.
cooldown.step3: Practica respiración abdominal profunda durante 1 minuto.
cooldown.step4: Termina con un estiramiento completo y una afirmación positiva.

tips.tip1: Bebe al menos 8 vasos de agua en días de entrenamiento. La hidratación previene calambres.
tips.tip2: El mejor momento para ejercitarte es cuando te sientes con más energía.
tips.tip3: ¡Los ejercicios de Kegel son invisibles pero poderosos! Practícalos todo el día.
tips.tip4: Caminar es uno de los ejercicios más seguros durante el embarazo. Apunta a 20-30 minutos diarios.
tips.tip5: La respiración correcta durante el ejercicio es clave. Nunca contengas la respiración.

boxBreathing.name: Box Breathing
boxBreathing.desc: A calming technique used by Navy SEALs

safePlace.name: Safe Place Visualization
steps: Close your eyes and take deep breaths
Imagine a place where you feel completely safe
Notice the colors, sounds, and smells
Feel the peace and comfort surrounding you
Stay here as long as you need

grounding54321.name: Anclaje 5-4-3-2-1
grounding54321.desc: A sensory technique to reduce anxiety

progressiveRelaxation.name: Progressive Muscle Relaxation
progressiveRelaxation.desc: Release tension from head to toe
steps: Start with your feet - tense for 5 seconds, release
Move to your calves - tense and release
Continue to thighs, abdomen, hands, arms
Tense and release shoulders, neck, face
Feel the wave of relaxation through your body

developments.week24: Los pulmones se desarrollan. Responde a sonidos y luz.
developments.week26: Los ojos comienzan a abrirse. El cerebro se desarrolla rápidamente.

developments.week30: El cerebro continúa creciendo rápidamente. Puede regular la temperatura corporal.
developments.week32: Practica la respiración. Los huesos están formados pero blandos.
developments.week34: Las uñas alcanzan las puntas de los dedos. Las capas de grasa se desarrollan.
developments.week36: Los pulmones están casi maduros. La cabeza puede encajarse en la pelvis.

evidence.title: ¿Qué es la preeclampsia?
evidence.content: La preeclampsia afecta al 3-8% de los embarazos a nivel mundial, caracterizada por presión arterial alta y proteína en la orina después de la semana 20. La detección temprana y el monitoreo regular reducen los riesgos hasta en un 60%.
evidence.source: Fuente: OMS — Recomendaciones para la prevención y el tratamiento de la preeclampsia, 2023

evidence2.title: Prevención y aspirina
evidence2.content: ACOG recomienda aspirina en dosis baja (81 mg diarios) a partir de las semanas 12-16 para mujeres de alto riesgo, reduciendo el riesgo de preeclampsia en un 24%. Consulta siempre a tu médico antes de tomar cualquier medicamento.
evidence2.source: Fuente: ACOG Practice Advisory — Aspirina en dosis baja para la prevención de preeclampsia, 2024

t1.detail: Intenta tomar 8 a 10 vasos de agua al día para mantener el líquido amniótico y reducir la hinchazón.

t2.detail: Las verduras como espinacas y col rizada son ricas en folato esencial para el desarrollo neural.

t3.detail: Caminar mejora la circulación, reduce la hinchazón y ayuda a preparar el parto.

t4.detail: Escribe 3 cosas por las que estés agradecida hoy. Reduce el estrés y mejora el ánimo.

t5.detail: ¡Tu bebé puede escucharte! Leer o cantar fortalece vuestro vínculo.

t6.detail: Esta posición mejora la circulación sanguínea hacia tu bebé y reduce la presión sobre los órganos.

t7.detail: El salmón, las nueces y las semillas de chía apoyan el desarrollo cerebral de tu bebé.

t8.detail: Los ejercicios del suelo pélvico te preparan para el parto y previenen la incontinencia.

t9.detail: Únete a una clase prenatal o comunidad online para apoyo y experiencias compartidas.

t10.detail: Después de la semana 28, controla los movimientos del bebé. Deberías sentir 10 patadas en 2 horas.

t11.detail: Reduce la hinchazón elevando los pies 15 minutos varias veces al día.

t12.detail: Un desayuno rico en proteínas estabiliza el azúcar en sangre y reduce las náuseas.

t13.detail: Los estiramientos suaves alivian el dolor de espalda y preparan tu cuerpo para el parto.

t14.detail: Aléjate de las pantallas durante 30 minutos para reducir el estrés y la fatiga visual.

t15.detail: Pon música relajante para el bebé – ¡la reconocerá después de nacer!

tips.carSeat: Busca una que se enganche al cochecito. Revisa la fecha de expiración.
tips.crib: Asegúrate de que cumpla con los estándares de seguridad actuales. Solo colchón firme.
tips.diapers: No compres demasiados talla recién nacido – ¡los bebés crecen rápido!

tips.stroller: Considera uno que acepte tu silla de auto para transiciones más fáciles.
tips.monitor: Solo audio suele ser suficiente. El video añade comodidad.
tips.breastPump: El seguro a menudo cubre esto. Revisa antes de comprar.
tips.swing: A algunos bebés les encanta, a otros no. Considera pedir prestado primero.
tips.diaperBag: Busca una con cambiador y múltiples compartimentos.
tips.bathtub: El lavabo también funciona bien para recién nacidos.
tips.swaddles: Las de muselina son transpirables y versátiles.
tips.thermometer: Los termómetros rectales digitales son más precisos para bebés.
tips.burpCloths: Consigue al menos 10-12. Muselina o rizo funcionan mejor.

fertilityTip.title: Consejo del día:
fertilityTip.disclaimer: Orientación general de bienestar — no sustituye el consejo médico profesional.

tips.tip1: La ventana fértil dura solo unos 6 días por ciclo — 5 días antes de la ovulación y el día mismo. Programar la intimidad en esta ventana aumenta significativamente tus posibilidades.
tips.tip2: El ácido fólico no es solo para el embarazo — es esencial para la calidad del óvulo. Comienza 400–800 µg diarios al menos 3 meses antes de intentar concebir.
tips.tip3: El estrés no solo afecta tu humor — puede retrasar o suprimir la ovulación por completo. Añade un ritual calmante: 5 minutos de respiración, caminata en la naturaleza o diario antes de dormir.
tips.tip4: Tu moco cervical cuenta una historia. Cuando se vuelve claro, elástico y resbaladizo — como clara de huevo cruda — tu cuerpo señala fertilidad máxima.
tips.tip5: El ejercicio moderado apoya la fertilidad, pero el entrenamiento excesivo puede suprimir la ovulación. Apunta a 30 minutos de movimiento suave 5 días a la semana.
tips.tip6: Ambos miembros de la pareja deben ser evaluados. En el 40% de los casos, el factor masculino juega un papel. Un simple espermiograma puede dar respuestas cruciales tempranamente.
tips.tip7: Tu tiroides es un regulador silencioso de la fertilidad. Incluso desequilibrios leves pueden afectar la ovulación. Pide a tu médico un test de TSH si no te has hecho uno recientemente.
tips.tip8: El sueño es cuando se producen y regulan tus hormonas reproductivas. Apunta a 7–9 horas de sueño de calidad. Mantén tu habitación fresca, oscura y sin pantallas 30 minutos antes.
tips.tip9: La deficiencia de vitamina D es sorprendentemente común y está vinculada a tasas de fertilidad más bajas. Pide un análisis de sangre y considera suplementar si tu nivel está bajo 30 ng/mL.
tips.tip10: La cafeína con moderación (menos de 200 mg/día) se considera generalmente segura. Pero reducir más puede apoyar la concepción, especialmente si llevas tiempo intentando.
tips.tip11: Tu peso importa para la fertilidad — tanto el bajo peso como el sobrepeso pueden alterar la ovulación. Incluso un cambio del 5% hacia un IMC saludable puede restaurar ciclos regulares.
tips.tip12: CoQ10 es un antioxidante que apoya la función mitocondrial en los óvulos. Para mujeres mayores de 35, 200–600 mg diarios puede mejorar la calidad. Consulta siempre a tu médico.
tips.tip13: No dependas solo de apps de seguimiento — predicen la ovulación basándose en promedios. Combina con observación del moco cervical y/o tiras de ovulación para mayor precisión.
tips.tip14: Después de la relación, no necesitas acostarte con las piernas elevadas — es un mito. Los espermatozoides llegan al cuello uterino en segundos. Simplemente relájate.
tips.tip15: Los omega-3 (especialmente DHA) apoyan la producción hormonal y reducen la inflamación. Come pescado graso como salmón dos veces por semana o toma un suplemento de calidad.
tips.tip16: Si llevas 12 meses intentando (o 6 si tienes más de 35) sin éxito, busca evaluación especializada. La evaluación temprana lleva a mejores resultados.
tips.tip17: Los lubricantes pueden reducir significativamente la movilidad espermática. Si necesitas lubricación, elige opciones específicamente diseñadas para no dañar los espermatozoides.
tips.tip18: El zinc es esencial para la calidad del óvulo y del espermatozoide. Incluye semillas de calabaza, ostras y garbanzos. Un suplemento de 8–15 mg diarios también es beneficioso.
tips.tip19: Tu bienestar emocional importa. Los caminos de fertilidad pueden ser agotadores emocionalmente. No dudes en buscar apoyo de un terapeuta, grupo de apoyo o amiga de confianza.
tips.tip20: El consumo de alcohol — incluso moderado — puede reducir la fertilidad en ambos miembros de la pareja. Considera reducir o eliminar el alcohol durante tu camino de concepción.
tips.tip21: La acupuntura ha mostrado resultados prometedores mejorando el flujo sanguíneo uterino y reduciendo hormonas del estrés. Muchas mujeres la encuentran un complemento útil.
tips.tip22: Las reservas de hierro deben optimizarse antes del embarazo. La ferritina baja es común y puede afectar la ovulación. Un simple análisis de sangre puede verificar tus niveles.
tips.tip23: Las toxinas ambientales en plásticos (BPA), pesticidas y productos de limpieza pueden actuar como disruptores endocrinos. Cambia a envases de vidrio y productos naturales.
tips.tip24: Rastrear tu temperatura basal por 3+ ciclos revela tu patrón de ovulación único. El aumento confirma la ovulación y te ayuda a planificar mejor los ciclos futuros.
tips.tip25: La hidratación afecta directamente la calidad del moco cervical. Apunta a 8–10 vasos de agua diarios. Las mujeres bien hidratadas producen más fluido cervical fértil.
tips.tip26: Los probióticos apoyan un microbioma vaginal saludable, importante para la supervivencia de espermatozoides y la implantación. Incluye alimentos fermentados o un probiótico con Lactobacillus.
tips.tip27: La edad afecta la calidad del óvulo más que la cantidad. No puedes detener el reloj, pero puedes proteger la calidad con antioxidantes, estilo de vida saludable y reducción del estrés oxidativo.
tips.tip28: La salud espermática de tu pareja también mejora con cambios de estilo de vida — dieta equilibrada, menos alcohol, no fumar y ropa interior holgada hacen diferencia medible en 3 meses.
tips.tip29: La paciencia es parte del proceso. Incluso con timing y salud perfectos, cada ciclo tiene aproximadamente 20–25% de probabilidad de concepción. La mayoría concibe en 6–12 meses.
tips.tip30: No estás sola. Una de cada seis parejas enfrenta dificultades. Buscar ayuda es señal de fortaleza.
tips.tip31: El selenio protege los óvulos del daño oxidativo y apoya la función tiroidea. Las nueces de Brasil son la fuente más rica — solo 1–2 al día.
tips.tip32: La fase lútea debería durar 12–14 días. Una fase corta puede indicar progesterona baja que impide la implantación.
tips.tip33: La endometriosis afecta a 1 de cada 10 mujeres. El diagnóstico temprano mejora significativamente los resultados.
tips.tip34: La vitamina B6 apoya la producción de progesterona. Alimentos ricos: garbanzos, plátanos, papas y aves.
tips.tip35: La salud del suelo pélvico impacta la fertilidad. Ejercicios suaves mejoran el flujo sanguíneo a los órganos reproductivos.
tips.tip36: El SOP es la causa más común de anovulación. Si tienes períodos irregulares o exceso de vello, consulta a tu médico.
tips.tip37: El magnesio apoya más de 300 reacciones enzimáticas. Buenas fuentes: chocolate oscuro, aguacate, nueces y vegetales de hoja verde.
tips.tip38: El inositol (especialmente mio-inositol) apoya la calidad ovocitaria y la regulación de insulina, especialmente beneficioso para mujeres con SOP. Dosis común: 2000–4000 mg diarios.
tips.tip39: El sueño profundo entre las 10 PM y 2 AM es crucial para la producción de melatonina, que protege los óvulos del daño oxidativo.
tips.tip40: Una ingesta adecuada de proteínas (aprox. 1g por kg de peso) apoya la producción de hormonas reproductivas. Varía entre fuentes animales y vegetales.
tips.tip41: La risa y la felicidad reducen el cortisol que suprime la ovulación. Dedica tiempo diario a algo que te haga genuinamente feliz.
tips.tip42: La prueba de AMH da información sobre tu reserva ovárica. Este simple análisis ayuda a planificar mejor con tu médico.
tips.tip43: 15–20 minutos de sol diario apoyan la producción natural de vitamina D y regulan tu ritmo circadiano vinculado a las hormonas reproductivas.
tips.tip44: Alimentos ricos en antioxidantes como bayas, granada y cúrcuma protegen óvulos y espermatozoides. Añade color a tu plato cada día.

toolsInternal.fertileWindow.lastPeriod: Primer día de su último período
toolsInternal.fertileWindow.disclaimer: Esta es una estimación basada en datos de ciclo promedio. La ovulación real puede variar. Consulte a su proveedor de atención médica para obtener orientación personalizada.

menstrualCycle.title: Understanding Your Menstrual Cycle
menstrualCycle.content:
Your menstrual cycle is divided into four main phases:

1. Menstrual Phase (Days 1–5): The uterine lining sheds, marking the start of a new cycle.

2. Follicular Phase (Days 1–13): Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) prompts the ovaries to develop follicles. Estrogen rises, thickening the uterine lining.

3. Ovulation (Day 14 approx.): A surge in luteinizing hormone (LH) triggers the release of a mature egg. This is your most fertile moment.

4. Luteal Phase (Days 15–28): The empty follicle becomes the corpus luteum, producing progesterone to maintain the lining. If fertilization doesn't occur, hormone levels drop and the cycle restarts.

Understanding these phases helps you identify your fertile window and work with your body's natural rhythm.

ovulationProcess.title: How Ovulation Works
ovulationProcess.content:
Ovulation is the release of a mature egg from one of your ovaries. Here's what happens:

• In the days leading up to ovulation, rising estrogen levels cause the dominant follicle to grow.
• A sharp LH surge triggers the follicle to rupture and release the egg into the fallopian tube.
• The egg survives 12–24 hours after release. Sperm can survive up to 5 days in the reproductive tract.
• This means your fertile window spans roughly 6 days — the 5 days before ovulation and the day of ovulation itself.

Some women feel a twinge or mild cramp (mittelschmerz) during ovulation. Others notice changes in cervical mucus, which becomes clear and stretchy like raw egg whites.

fertilizationBasics.title: Fertilization & Implantation
fertilizationBasics.content:
After ovulation, fertilization can occur in the fallopian tube:

• Of the millions of sperm released, only a few hundred reach the egg.
• One sperm penetrates the egg's outer layer, triggering a chemical reaction that prevents other sperm from entering.
• The fertilized egg (zygote) begins dividing as it travels toward the uterus over 3–4 days.
• Around day 6–10 after ovulation, the blastocyst implants into the uterine lining.
• Implantation may cause light spotting, known as implantation bleeding.
• Once implanted, the body begins producing hCG — the hormone detected by pregnancy tests.

hormonesRole.title: Key Fertility Hormones
hormonesRole.content:
Several hormones orchestrate your fertility:

• FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone): Stimulates follicle growth in the ovaries. Tested on Day 3 of your cycle to assess ovarian reserve.

• LH (Luteinizing Hormone): Triggers ovulation. Ovulation predictor kits detect the LH surge.

• Estrogen (Estradiol): Promotes follicle development and thickens the uterine lining. Also affects cervical mucus quality.

• Progesterone: Produced after ovulation, it maintains the uterine lining for potential implantation. Low progesterone can affect cycle regularity.

• AMH (Anti-Müllerian Hormone): Reflects the number of remaining eggs (ovarian reserve). Useful for fertility planning.

• Thyroid Hormones (TSH, T3, T4): Thyroid imbalances can disrupt ovulation and cycle regularity.

ageAndFertility.title: Age & Fertility
ageAndFertility.content:
Age is one of the most significant factors affecting fertility:

• Women are born with all the eggs they will ever have — about 1–2 million at birth.
• By puberty, roughly 300,000–400,000 eggs remain. Only about 300–400 will ovulate during a lifetime.
• Fertility peaks in the mid-20s. A gradual decline begins after age 30.
• After 35, the decline accelerates. Egg quality and quantity both decrease.
• By age 40, the chance of natural conception per cycle drops to about 5%.
• Male fertility also declines with age, though more gradually.

This doesn't mean pregnancy after 35 is impossible — many women conceive naturally. But understanding timing helps with planning.

lifestyleFactors.title: Lifestyle Factors That Affect Fertility
lifestyleFactors.content:
Your daily habits significantly impact fertility:

• Weight: Both underweight (BMI < 18.5) and overweight (BMI > 30) can disrupt ovulation. Even a 5–10% weight change can improve cycle regularity.

• Smoking: Reduces egg quality, damages the fallopian tubes, and accelerates egg loss. Secondhand smoke is also harmful.

• Alcohol: Heavy drinking is linked to irregular cycles and reduced fertility. Moderate to no alcohol is safest when trying to conceive.

• Caffeine: More than 200–300mg daily (about 2 cups of coffee) may slightly reduce fertility.

• Exercise: Moderate activity supports fertility. Excessive intense exercise can disrupt ovulation.

• Sleep: Poor sleep disrupts reproductive hormones. Aim for 7–9 hours per night.

• Environmental Toxins: Limit exposure to pesticides, heavy metals, and certain plastics (BPA).

commonMyths.title: Fertility Myths vs. Facts
commonMyths.content:
Let's separate fact from fiction:

Myth: You can only get pregnant on ovulation day.
Fact: The fertile window spans about 6 days. Sperm can survive up to 5 days.

Myth: Position during intercourse affects conception.
Fact: No scientific evidence supports this. Sperm reach the cervix within seconds.

Myth: Birth control causes infertility.
Fact: Fertility typically returns within 1–3 months after stopping most contraceptives.

Myth: Stress directly causes infertility.
Fact: While severe chronic stress can affect hormones, moderate stress alone rarely prevents conception.

Myth: You need to have intercourse every day.
Fact: Every 1–2 days during the fertile window is sufficient. Daily intercourse doesn't reduce sperm quality.

Myth: Herbal supplements guarantee fertility.
Fact: Some may help, but none are scientifically proven to guarantee conception. Always consult your doctor.

whenToSeekHelp.title: When to Consult a Specialist
whenToSeekHelp.content:
Consider seeking professional fertility guidance if:

• You're under 35 and have been trying for 12 months without success.
• You're 35–39 and have been trying for 6 months.
• You're 40 or older — consult early, as time is a factor.
• You have irregular or absent periods.
• You have a known condition: endometriosis, PCOS, thyroid disorder, or previous pelvic surgery.
• You've experienced recurrent miscarriages (2 or more).
• Your partner has known fertility concerns.

A fertility specialist can run tests (hormone panels, ultrasound, semen analysis) and discuss options ranging from lifestyle changes to assisted reproduction.

maleFertility.title: Male Fertility Essentials
maleFertility.content:
Male fertility is equally important in the conception journey:

• Sperm production takes about 74 days. Lifestyle changes need time to show results.
• Optimal sperm health requires: avoiding excessive heat (hot tubs, laptops on lap), maintaining a healthy weight, and managing stress.
• Factors that can reduce sperm quality: smoking, excessive alcohol, certain medications, anabolic steroids, and recreational drugs.
• A semen analysis is a simple, non-invasive test that evaluates sperm count, motility, and morphology.
• Age affects male fertility too — sperm quality gradually declines after age 40.
• Key nutrients for sperm health: zinc, selenium, vitamin C, vitamin E, folate, and omega-3 fatty acids.

emotionalWellbeing.title: Emotional Wellbeing on the Journey
emotionalWellbeing.content:
Trying to conceive can be an emotional rollercoaster:

• It's normal to feel hope, excitement, frustration, and sadness — sometimes all in one cycle.
• About 1 in 6 couples experience difficulty conceiving. You are not alone.
• Grief after a negative test is valid. Allow yourself to feel without judgment.
• Communication with your partner is essential. Share your feelings openly.
• Consider joining a support community — connecting with others on the same journey can be comforting.
• Set boundaries around pregnancy announcements and baby showers if they feel triggering.
• Professional counseling can provide coping strategies tailored to your situation.
• Remember: your worth is not defined by your fertility. Be kind to yourself.

trackingMethods.title: Fertility Tracking Methods
trackingMethods.content:
Several methods can help you identify your fertile window:

1. Calendar Method: Track cycle lengths over several months. Ovulation typically occurs 14 days before your next period.

2. Basal Body Temperature (BBT): Take your temperature every morning before getting up. A slight rise (0.2–0.5°C) confirms ovulation has occurred.

3. Cervical Mucus Observation: As ovulation approaches, mucus becomes clear, stretchy, and slippery (like raw egg white).

4. Ovulation Predictor Kits (OPKs): Detect the LH surge in urine 12–36 hours before ovulation.

5. Fertility Monitors: Advanced devices that track multiple hormones for a more complete picture.

6. Cervical Position: The cervix becomes higher, softer, and more open around ovulation.

Combining multiple methods increases accuracy.

optimizingTiming.title: Optimizing Timing for Conception
optimizingTiming.content:
Timing is crucial for maximizing your chances:

• The best days for conception are the 2–3 days before ovulation and ovulation day itself.
• Having intercourse every 1–2 days during the fertile window provides the best results.
• Sperm quality is not significantly reduced by daily ejaculation in most men.
• After intercourse, lying down for 10–15 minutes may slightly improve sperm transport, though evidence is limited.
• Lubricants: Some commercial lubricants can reduce sperm motility. Use fertility-friendly brands if needed.
• Track your cycle for 2–3 months before actively trying, to understand your personal pattern.
• Stay hydrated and maintain a balanced diet during the fertile window.
• Both partners should avoid excessive alcohol during the fertile window.

implantationWindow.title: The Implantation Window
implantationWindow.content:
The implantation window is the brief period when the uterine lining is ready to receive the embryo:

• Typically occurs between days 6–10 after ovulation.
• During this time, the endometrium secretes special proteins that help the embryo attach.
• Progesterone plays a central role in preparing the lining.
• Factors affecting window quality: chronic inflammation, insufficient lining thickness, or immune disorders.
• Rest and good nutrition during the luteal phase support the implantation process.

cervicalHealth.title: Cervical Health & Fertility
cervicalHealth.content:
The cervix plays a fundamental role in fertility:

• It produces mucus that changes throughout the cycle to facilitate or block sperm passage.
• Fertile mucus nourishes sperm and extends their lifespan up to 5 days.
• Chronic cervical infections may reduce mucus quality.
• Previous cervical procedures (like cone biopsies) may affect mucus production.
• Maintaining healthy vaginal pH supports the optimal cervical environment for fertility.

thyroidFertility.title: Thyroid & Fertility
thyroidFertility.content:
Thyroid disorders are among the most treatable causes of fertility problems:

• Hypothyroidism can cause irregular cycles, absent ovulation, or early miscarriage.
• Hyperthyroidism is linked to irregular periods and difficulty conceiving.
• Even subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH 2.5–4.0) may affect fertility.
• Thyroid antibodies (Anti-TPO) are common and may impact implantation.
• TSH and FT4 testing is recommended for all women planning pregnancy.
• Levothyroxine treatment is safe and effective while trying to conceive.

pcosManagement.title: Managing PCOS & Fertility
pcosManagement.content:
Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) affects 8–13% of women:

• The most common cause of anovulation and ovulation-related infertility.
• Key symptoms: irregular periods, acne, excess hair growth, insulin resistance.
• Lifestyle changes are very effective: losing 5–10% of weight can restore ovulation.
• Inositol has shown promising results in improving insulin sensitivity and egg quality.
• A low-sugar, balanced diet supports hormonal regularity.
• Medications like clomiphene or letrozole successfully stimulate ovulation in many cases.

endometriosisAwareness.title: Endometriosis & Fertility
endometriosisAwareness.content:
Endometriosis affects 1 in 10 women:

• Tissue similar to the uterine lining grows outside the uterus, causing inflammation and adhesions.
• It can affect the tubes, ovaries, and egg quality.
• Symptoms: severe period pain, pain during intercourse, and sometimes no symptoms.
• Early diagnosis improves outcomes. Laparoscopy is the gold standard.
• Many affected women conceive naturally or with assistance.
• Consult a fertility specialist if you have a family history or suspicious symptoms.

nutritionFertility.title: Optimal Nutrition for Fertility
nutritionFertility.content:
Diet directly affects egg and sperm quality:

• The Mediterranean diet has shown the best results in fertility studies.
• Healthy fats (omega-3, olive oil) support hormone production.
• Plant protein may be better than animal protein for some women.
• Avoid trans fats, which double the risk of ovulation-related infertility.
• Organic foods reduce exposure to hormone-disrupting pesticides.
• Plant-source iron + vitamin C significantly improves absorption.

sleepHormones.title: Sleep & Fertility Hormones
sleepHormones.content:
Sleep is not a luxury — it's a biological necessity for fertility:

• Melatonin (sleep hormone) acts as an antioxidant protecting eggs.
• Less than 6 hours of sleep raises cortisol, which suppresses GnRH and ovulation.
• Shift work is associated with increased cycle and fertility problems.
• The circadian rhythm regulates FSH and LH secretion.
• Blue light from screens suppresses melatonin and disrupts rhythm.
• Goal: 7–9 hours of sleep in a dark, cool room with a consistent routine.

exerciseBalance.title: Balancing Exercise & Fertility
exerciseBalance.content:
Exercise is a double-edged sword for fertility:

• Moderate activity (150 minutes/week) improves ovulation and fertility.
• Continuous intense exercise can cause 'relative energy deficiency' stopping ovulation.
• Walking, swimming, and yoga are among the best options.
• Moderate strength training improves insulin sensitivity (helpful for PCOS).
• Fertility yoga combines movement with stress reduction.
• If your period disappears with intense exercise, reduce intensity immediately.
• The balance between movement and rest is key.

supplementsGuide.title: Smart Supplements Guide
supplementsGuide.content:
Supplements can support your fertility — but not all are equal:

• Folic acid/methylfolate: Essential — start 3 months before trying.
• CoQ10 (200–600mg): Supports egg energy, especially after 35.
• Vitamin D (1000–2000 IU): Most women are deficient.
• Omega-3 DHA (200–300mg): For hormone health and inflammation.
• Inositol (4g myo + 100mg d-chiro): Especially helpful with PCOS.
• Iron: Only if tests show deficiency — excess is harmful.
• Always consult your doctor before starting any new supplement.

environmentalToxins.title: Environmental Toxins & Fertility
environmentalToxins.content:
Daily exposure to environmental toxins can affect your fertility:

• BPA in plastics mimics estrogen and disrupts hormonal balance.
• Phthalates in cosmetics and fragrances disrupt the endocrine system.
• Pesticides on fruits and vegetables are linked to lower egg quality.
• Heavy metals (lead, mercury) affect both eggs and sperm.
• Practical steps: glass containers instead of plastic, natural cleaning products, wash fruits well.
• Filtering drinking water reduces pollutant exposure.

geneticFactors.title: Genetic Factors & Fertility
geneticFactors.content:
Genes play a bigger role than expected in fertility:

• Carrier genetic screening reveals risk of passing genetic diseases to children.
• MTHFR mutation affects folate metabolism — methylfolate may be better.
• Turner syndrome and chromosomal abnormalities affect ovarian reserve.
• Family history of early menopause may signal early reserve decline.
• Karyotype testing is recommended in cases of recurrent miscarriage.
• Genetic counseling is available and useful for those with specific family histories.

immunologyFertility.title: Immunology & Fertility
immunologyFertility.content:
The immune system plays a delicate role in pregnancy:

• The body must accept the embryo (half its genes from the father) without rejecting it.
• Uterine NK cells help with implantation — but excessive activity may hinder it.
• Antiphospholipid antibodies are linked to recurrent miscarriage.
• Autoimmune diseases (lupus, Hashimoto's) may affect fertility.
• Chronic pelvic inflammation can damage the tubes.
• An anti-inflammatory lifestyle (nutrition, sleep, movement) supports balanced immunity.

secondaryInfertility.title: Secondary Infertility
secondaryInfertility.content:
Secondary infertility means difficulty conceiving after having a previous child:

• Accounts for about 50% of infertility cases — but is less discussed.
• Causes: advancing age, weight changes, complications from previous pregnancy.
• Adhesions from cesarean section or infection may affect fertility.
• Hormonal changes after first birth may not fully normalize.
• Social expectations ('you have a child, be grateful') increase psychological burden.
• Don't delay seeking help — the same time criteria apply.

recurrentLoss.title: Understanding Recurrent Pregnancy Loss
recurrentLoss.content:
Recurrent pregnancy loss (two or more) requires comprehensive evaluation:

• 15–20% of pregnancies end in miscarriage — most due to chromosomal abnormalities.
• Treatable causes: clotting disorders, uterine anomalies, thyroid problems.
• Uterine septum is the most common uterine anomaly and can be surgically corrected.
• Antiphospholipid syndrome is treatable with aspirin and heparin.
• Psychological support is essential — grief and anxiety after loss are natural and valid.
• 60–70% of couples with unexplained recurrent loss succeed in the next pregnancy.

fertilityPreservation.title: Fertility Preservation
fertilityPreservation.content:
Fertility preservation is an available option that gives you flexibility in planning:

• Egg freezing: Ideal age before 35. Excellent results with modern techniques.
• Embryo freezing: Option for married couples. Higher success rates than eggs alone.
• Reasons for freezing: delayed childbearing, chemotherapy, medical conditions.
• The process requires hormonal stimulation for 10–14 days then egg retrieval.
• Cost and storage are factors to consider.
• Consult a fertility specialist to assess if it's suitable for your situation.

assistedReproduction.title: Assisted Reproduction Techniques
assistedReproduction.content:
When natural conception doesn't succeed, there are effective options:

• Ovulation stimulation: Medications that stimulate egg production — first-line treatment.
• Intrauterine insemination (IUI): Processed sperm injected directly into the uterus.
• In vitro fertilization (IVF): The most advanced technique. Success rates 30–50% per cycle.
• Intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI): A single sperm injected directly into the egg.
• Preimplantation genetic testing (PGT): Screening embryos for chromosomal abnormalities.
• Each option has specific indications — a fertility specialist guides you to the most suitable.

epigeneticsFertility.title: Epigenetics & Generational Health
epigeneticsFertility.content:
Epigenetics discoveries have changed our understanding of fertility:

• Your nutrition and lifestyle can influence gene expression in your future child.
• Folate deficiency before pregnancy affects fetal gene programming.
• Chronic stress can leave 'imprints' on DNA that transfer to the embryo.
• Pollution and environmental toxins cause epigenetic changes that may affect generations.
• Good news: negative epigenetic changes are reversible with a healthy lifestyle.
• 3–6 months of improvements before pregnancy make a real difference in fetal health.

mindBodyConnection.title: The Mind-Body Connection
mindBodyConnection.content:
The relationship between mind and body is deeper than we imagine in fertility:

• Chronic stress activates the HPA axis which suppresses reproductive hormones.
• Relaxation techniques (meditation, yoga, deep breathing) measurably improve fertility outcomes.
• Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) has shown positive results in reducing fertility anxiety.
• Positive visualization and affirmations activate neural pathways that support hormonal balance.
• Support groups reduce feelings of isolation and improve mental health.
• Art, music, and creative writing are effective therapeutic tools during the fertility journey.
• Seek professional help if anxiety or sadness begin to dominate your life.

cervicalMucus.title: Cervical Mucus Changes
cervicalMucus.description: As ovulation approaches, cervical mucus becomes more abundant, clear, and stretchy — resembling raw egg whites. This 'fertile-quality' mucus helps sperm travel more easily through the cervix. After ovulation, mucus typically becomes thicker, cloudier, or dries up.
cervicalMucus.tip: Check mucus daily by wiping before urination. The most fertile-type mucus stretches between your fingers without breaking.

basalTemp.title: Basal Body Temperature Shift
basalTemp.description: After ovulation, progesterone causes your resting body temperature to rise by 0.2–0.5°C. This shift confirms that ovulation has already occurred. By tracking BBT over several cycles, you can identify your typical ovulation pattern.
basalTemp.tip: Take your temperature every morning at the same time, before getting out of bed. Use a BBT-specific thermometer for accuracy.

cervicalPosition.title: Cervical Position Changes
cervicalPosition.description: During your fertile window, the cervix moves higher in the vaginal canal, becomes softer (like your lips vs. the tip of your nose), and opens slightly. After ovulation, it drops lower, firms up, and closes.
cervicalPosition.tip: Check cervical position in the same position daily (e.g., standing with one foot elevated). It takes practice — be patient with yourself.

ovulationPain.title: Ovulation Sensation (Mittelschmerz)
ovulationPain.description: Some women feel a mild twinge, cramp, or ache on one side of the lower abdomen during ovulation. This 'middle pain' (mittelschmerz) is caused by the follicle rupturing to release the egg. It typically lasts a few minutes to a few hours.
ovulationPain.tip: Note which side you feel the sensation — it may alternate sides each cycle as ovulation switches between ovaries.

breastTenderness.title: Breast Tenderness
breastTenderness.description: Rising progesterone after ovulation can cause breast tenderness, fullness, or sensitivity. This is one of the most commonly noticed luteal phase symptoms. Some women also experience this in the days leading up to ovulation due to rising estrogen.
breastTenderness.tip: Track when tenderness begins and ends to correlate it with your ovulation pattern over time.

libidoChanges.title: Changes in Desire
libidoChanges.description: Many women experience a natural increase in desire around ovulation. This is driven by the surge in estrogen and testosterone that precedes the LH peak. It's one of the body's ways of optimizing the chance of conception.
libidoChanges.tip: Pay attention to these natural shifts — they often align with your most fertile days.

moodShifts.title: Mood & Energy Shifts
moodShifts.description: Estrogen rising in the follicular phase often brings improved mood, energy, and confidence. After ovulation, progesterone dominance may cause a shift toward calmness or, for some, irritability and fatigue. These fluctuations are normal and hormone-driven.
moodShifts.tip: Journaling your mood daily can reveal patterns that correlate with your cycle phases.

bloating.title: Mild Bloating
bloating.description: Hormonal changes around ovulation, particularly rising progesterone, can cause water retention and mild bloating. This is a common sign that your body is transitioning into the luteal phase.
bloating.tip: Staying hydrated and reducing sodium intake can help manage ovulation-related bloating.

lightSpotting.title: Ovulation Spotting
lightSpotting.description: A small amount of light pink or brown spotting around ovulation day is experienced by some women. It's caused by the slight drop in estrogen that occurs just before the LH surge, or by the follicle rupture itself. It's usually very light and brief.
lightSpotting.tip: Ovulation spotting is different from implantation bleeding (which occurs 6–12 days after ovulation). Track the timing carefully.

heightenedSenses.title: Heightened Senses
heightenedSenses.description: Some women report a more acute sense of smell during ovulation, possibly due to elevated estrogen levels. Research suggests this may be an evolutionary adaptation. Some also notice subtle changes in taste preferences.
heightenedSenses.tip: If you notice scents seem stronger around mid-cycle, it may be a personal ovulation indicator worth tracking.

folateRich.title: Folate-Rich Foods
folateRich.description: Folate (vitamin B9) is critical for preventing neural tube defects and supporting healthy cell division. The recommended daily intake when trying to conceive is 400–800mcg. Start folate supplementation at least 1–3 months before conception.
folateRich.foods: Dark leafy greens (spinach, kale), lentils, chickpeas, asparagus, broccoli, avocado, fortified cereals, citrus fruits, beets, Brussels sprouts

ironSources.title: Iron-Rich Foods
ironSources.description: Iron supports healthy blood production and oxygen transport, which are essential for a developing pregnancy. Women of childbearing age need about 18mg daily. Iron deficiency is common and can affect ovulation and cycle regularity.
ironSources.foods: Red meat, poultry, fish, lentils, spinach, fortified cereals, pumpkin seeds, tofu, dark chocolate, quinoa, dried apricots

omega3.title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids
omega3.description: Omega-3s support hormone production, reduce inflammation, and are essential for fetal brain and eye development. DHA and EPA are the most bioavailable forms. ALA from plant sources is also beneficial but converts less efficiently.
omega3.foods: Salmon, sardines, mackerel, walnuts, chia seeds, flaxseeds, hemp seeds, algae-based DHA supplements, fortified eggs

antioxidants.title: Antioxidant-Rich Foods
antioxidants.description: Antioxidants protect eggs and sperm from oxidative stress caused by free radicals. Vitamins C and E, selenium, and CoQ10 are particularly important. A colorful, varied diet provides a broad spectrum of antioxidants.
antioxidants.foods: Berries (blueberries, strawberries, raspberries), tomatoes, bell peppers, nuts, seeds, green tea, dark leafy greens, sweet potatoes, carrots

zinc.title: Zinc Sources
zinc.description: Zinc is essential for hormone balance, egg quality, and cell division. It also plays a crucial role in male fertility — supporting sperm production and testosterone levels. The recommended daily intake is 8mg for women.
zinc.foods: Oysters, beef, pumpkin seeds, cashews, chickpeas, lentils, yogurt, cheese, whole grains, dark chocolate

vitaminD.title: Vitamin D Sources
vitaminD.description: Vitamin D receptors are found in the ovaries and uterus, suggesting a direct role in fertility. Deficiency is associated with irregular cycles, PCOS, and reduced IVF success rates. Many people are deficient, especially in northern climates.
vitaminD.foods: Sunlight exposure (15–20 min daily), fatty fish (salmon, tuna), egg yolks, fortified milk, mushrooms exposed to UV light, cod liver oil

calcium.title: Calcium Sources
calcium.description: Calcium is vital for reproductive function, supporting the development of embryonic structures. Adequate calcium intake before pregnancy helps build reserves for the baby's skeletal development. Aim for 1000mg daily.
calcium.foods: Dairy products (milk, yogurt, cheese), fortified plant milks, kale, broccoli, almonds, sardines with bones, tofu, figs

protein.title: Quality Protein
protein.description: Protein provides the building blocks for hormones and new cells. Research suggests that replacing some animal protein with plant protein may improve fertility outcomes. Aim for a balanced mix of both sources.
protein.foods: Eggs, lean poultry, fish, legumes (beans, lentils), nuts, seeds, Greek yogurt, tofu, tempeh, quinoa

hydration.title: Hydration
hydration.description: Water is essential for cervical mucus production, nutrient transport, and hormone balance. Dehydration can reduce cervical mucus quality and affect blood flow to the reproductive organs. Aim for 8–10 glasses daily.
hydration.foods: Water, herbal teas (raspberry leaf, ginger), coconut water, water-rich fruits (watermelon, cucumber, oranges), broths

avoidList.title: Foods to Minimize
avoidList.description: Certain foods and substances may negatively impact fertility. While occasional consumption is unlikely to cause harm, reducing these when actively trying to conceive can optimize your body's readiness.
avoidList.foods: Excessive caffeine (>200mg/day), alcohol, trans fats, highly processed foods, raw fish (sushi), high-mercury fish (swordfish, king mackerel), excessive sugar, artificial sweeteners

folicAcid.title: Folic Acid (Vitamin B9)
folicAcid.description: The most important preconception supplement. Folic acid significantly reduces the risk of neural tube defects (spina bifida, anencephaly). Start taking it at least 1–3 months before trying to conceive for maximum benefit.
folicAcid.dosage: Recommended: 400–800mcg daily. Some women (with history of NTDs, on certain medications) may need 4–5mg — consult your doctor.

iron.title: Iron
iron.description: Iron requirements increase dramatically during pregnancy (from 18mg to 27mg daily). Building iron stores before conception helps prevent iron-deficiency anemia, which is associated with preterm birth and low birth weight.
iron.dosage: Recommended: 18mg daily pre-pregnancy. Take with vitamin C to improve absorption. Avoid taking with calcium or tea.

vitaminD.title: Vitamin D
vitaminD.description: Vitamin D supports immune function, calcium absorption, and reproductive health. Deficiency is linked to pregnancy complications including gestational diabetes and preeclampsia. Most people need supplementation, especially in winter months.
vitaminD.dosage: Recommended: 600–2000 IU daily. Your doctor may test your levels and recommend a higher dose if deficient.

omega3DHA.title: Omega-3 DHA
omega3DHA.description: DHA is crucial for fetal brain and retinal development. Starting supplementation before pregnancy ensures adequate stores from the earliest stages of embryonic development. EPA also supports maternal cardiovascular health.
omega3DHA.dosage: Recommended: 200–300mg DHA daily. Choose supplements tested for mercury and contaminants.

calcium.title: Calcium
calcium.description: If the mother's calcium intake is insufficient during pregnancy, the baby will draw calcium from her bones. Building strong calcium reserves before pregnancy protects maternal bone health.
calcium.dosage: Recommended: 1000mg daily from food and supplements combined. Best absorbed in doses of 500mg or less at a time.

iodine.title: Iodine
iodine.description: Iodine is essential for thyroid function, which directly impacts fertility and fetal brain development. Deficiency is the leading preventable cause of intellectual disability worldwide.
iodine.dosage: Recommended: 150mcg daily before pregnancy, 220mcg during pregnancy. Check that your prenatal vitamin includes iodine.

zinc.title: Zinc
zinc.description: Zinc supports egg quality, hormone balance, and immune function. It also plays a key role in DNA synthesis and cell division — critical processes in early pregnancy.
zinc.dosage: Recommended: 8mg daily for women. Most prenatal vitamins contain adequate zinc.

vitaminB12.title: Vitamin B12
vitaminB12.description: B12 works with folate to support DNA synthesis and nervous system development. Deficiency is more common in vegetarians and vegans. Low B12 levels are associated with recurrent miscarriage and neural tube defects.
vitaminB12.dosage: Recommended: 2.4mcg daily. Vegans should supplement as B12 is found primarily in animal products.

vitaminC.title: Vitamin C
vitaminC.description: Vitamin C is a powerful antioxidant that protects eggs from oxidative damage. It also enhances iron absorption and supports immune function. Progesterone levels may also benefit from adequate vitamin C intake.
vitaminC.dosage: Recommended: 75–90mg daily. Easily obtained from citrus fruits, berries, and bell peppers.

vitaminE.title: Vitamin E
vitaminE.description: Vitamin E supports endometrial lining thickness and quality, which is important for implantation. It also protects cell membranes from oxidative stress and supports overall reproductive health.
vitaminE.dosage: Recommended: 15mg (22 IU) daily. Found in nuts, seeds, and vegetable oils.

coq10.title: CoQ10 (Coenzyme Q10)
coq10.description: CoQ10 is an antioxidant that supports mitochondrial function in eggs. As egg quality naturally declines with age, CoQ10 supplementation may help maintain cellular energy production. Research is ongoing but promising, especially for women over 35.
coq10.dosage: Common dosage: 100–600mg daily. Ubiquinol form is better absorbed than ubiquinone. Consult your doctor.

probiotics.title: Probiotics
probiotics.description: A healthy vaginal and gut microbiome supports fertility. Lactobacillus strains help maintain optimal vaginal pH, which is important for sperm survival. Gut health also affects hormone metabolism and nutrient absorption.
probiotics.dosage: Choose a multi-strain probiotic with Lactobacillus strains. Consume fermented foods: yogurt, kefir, sauerkraut, kimchi.

toolsInternal.twwCompanion.subtitle: Su guía diaria durante la espera de dos semanas

day1.title: Ovulation Day + 1
day1.body: If ovulation occurred yesterday, the egg is now traveling through the fallopian tube. If fertilization happens, it typically occurs within 12–24 hours of ovulation. Today, there's nothing you can do to influence the outcome — and that's okay.
day1.tip: Take a deep breath. The journey has begun. Focus on something you enjoy today.

day2.title: Cellular Beginnings
day2.body: If fertilization occurred, the single-celled zygote begins dividing — first into 2 cells, then 4. This tiny cluster of cells is traveling through the fallopian tube toward the uterus. It's powered entirely by its own energy stores.
day2.tip: Stay hydrated and eat nourishing foods. Your body is doing incredible work at the cellular level.

day3.title: The Morula Stage
day3.body: The dividing cells form a solid ball called a morula (about 16 cells). It continues its journey through the fallopian tube. Progesterone is rising, preparing the uterine lining for potential implantation.
day3.tip: Gentle activity is perfectly fine. There's no need to restrict your normal routine.

day4.title: Entering the Uterus
day4.body: The morula typically enters the uterus around day 4. It begins forming a fluid-filled cavity, transitioning into a blastocyst. The uterine lining is becoming thick and receptive under progesterone's influence.
day4.tip: Practice mindfulness or light stretching. Managing anticipation is part of the journey.

day5.title: Blastocyst Formation
day5.body: The blastocyst now has two distinct cell groups: the inner cell mass (which becomes the embryo) and the trophoblast (which becomes the placenta). It floats freely in the uterine cavity.
day5.tip: This is a good day to journal your thoughts and feelings. Self-expression is therapeutic.

day6.title: Implantation May Begin
day6.body: The blastocyst begins searching for an ideal implantation site on the uterine wall. The trophoblast cells start burrowing into the endometrium. Some women experience light spotting (implantation bleeding) around this time.
day6.tip: Light spotting at this stage can be normal. Don't panic — and don't test yet. It's too early.

day7.title: Implantation Window
day7.body: Days 6–10 are the typical implantation window. The blastocyst is embedding deeper into the uterine lining. Early signals are being exchanged between the embryo and your body.
day7.tip: Continue your normal activities. Rest when you need to. Trust the process.

day8.title: hCG Begins
day8.body: If implantation is progressing, tiny amounts of hCG (human chorionic gonadotropin) begin to be produced. This hormone signals your body to maintain the corpus luteum, which keeps producing progesterone. Levels are still too low to detect on a test.
day8.tip: Resist the urge to test. hCG needs time to build. Patience is your greatest ally right now.

day9.title: Rising Signals
day9.body: hCG levels are rising but may still be below the detection threshold of most home pregnancy tests. Implantation should be complete or nearly complete. Your body is starting to respond to the new hormonal signals.
day9.tip: Treat yourself to something calming — a warm bath, your favorite tea, or a walk in nature.

day10.title: Almost There
day10.body: By now, if implantation occurred, hCG levels are doubling roughly every 48 hours. Some sensitive tests might detect very early pregnancy. However, a negative result today doesn't mean you're not pregnant — levels vary widely.
day10.tip: If you test today and it's negative, don't lose hope. Many successful pregnancies don't show positive until day 12–14.

day11.title: Building Anticipation
day11.body: hCG continues to rise. Some women begin to notice very early symptoms: mild cramping, breast tenderness, or fatigue. These symptoms can also be caused by progesterone in a non-pregnant cycle, so they're not reliable indicators.
day11.tip: Symptom-spotting is natural but can increase anxiety. Try to stay present and engaged in your daily life.

day12.title: Testing Becomes More Reliable
day12.body: Most home pregnancy tests become more reliable from today onward. If testing, use first morning urine for the highest concentration of hCG. A faint line is still a positive result.
day12.tip: Whatever the result today, remember: one cycle does not define your journey. Be gentle with yourself.

day13.title: The Eve of Knowing
day13.body: Tomorrow is typically the day your period would be expected (in a 28-day cycle). If it doesn't arrive and a test shows a faint positive, congratulations may be in order. If your period comes, allow yourself to grieve and know that each cycle is a new opportunity.
day13.tip: Prepare emotionally for both outcomes. Both are valid. Both are part of the journey.

day14.title: The Answer Day
day14.body: This is typically the day your period is expected. A missed period combined with a positive pregnancy test is a strong indicator of pregnancy. If your test is positive, contact your healthcare provider to schedule your first appointment (usually around 6–8 weeks).
day14.tip: Positive? Celebrate this moment. Negative? You are strong, resilient, and this journey continues. Both outcomes deserve compassion.

stressHormones.title: How Stress Affects Fertility
stressHormones.content: Chronic stress triggers the release of cortisol and adrenaline, which can disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis — the hormonal pathway that controls ovulation. Elevated cortisol may suppress GnRH (gonadotropin-releasing hormone), leading to irregular or absent ovulation. While moderate daily stress is unlikely to prevent conception, prolonged high stress can delay time to pregnancy.
stressHormones.practice: Identify your top 3 stress triggers this week. For each one, write down one small action you can take to reduce its impact.

cortisol.title: Understanding Cortisol
cortisol.content: Cortisol is your body's primary stress hormone. In small bursts, it's helpful — providing energy and focus. But when cortisol remains chronically elevated, it can suppress reproductive hormones, disrupt sleep, increase inflammation, and affect mood. The goal isn't to eliminate stress entirely (impossible!) but to manage your stress response.
cortisol.practice: Practice the 4-7-8 breathing technique: Inhale for 4 seconds, hold for 7, exhale for 8. Repeat 4 times. This activates the parasympathetic nervous system.

mindfulness.title: Mindfulness for Fertility
mindfulness.content: Mindfulness is the practice of being fully present without judgment. Studies show that mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) programs can reduce anxiety and depression in women undergoing fertility treatment, and may improve outcomes. Even 10 minutes of daily mindfulness practice can lower cortisol levels and improve emotional resilience.
mindfulness.practice: Try a 5-minute body scan: Close your eyes, breathe deeply, and slowly move your attention from your toes to your head, noticing sensations without trying to change them.

breathing.title: Breathing Techniques
breathing.content: Controlled breathing directly activates the vagus nerve, triggering the relaxation response. This lowers heart rate, blood pressure, and cortisol. It's one of the fastest ways to shift from a stress state to a calm state. Different techniques serve different purposes — some energize, others calm.
breathing.practice: Box Breathing: Inhale 4 counts → Hold 4 counts → Exhale 4 counts → Hold 4 counts. Repeat for 5 minutes. Ideal before bed or during anxious moments.

journaling.title: Therapeutic Journaling
journaling.content: Writing about your emotions has been scientifically shown to reduce stress, improve immune function, and provide clarity. For fertility journaling, the goal isn't to track symptoms obsessively, but to process emotions, celebrate small wins, and release fears.
journaling.practice: Tonight, write down: 1) One thing I'm grateful for today, 2) One worry I want to release, 3) One kind thing I did for myself.

sleep.title: Sleep & Fertility
sleep.content: Sleep is when your body repairs, produces hormones, and processes emotions. Poor sleep disrupts melatonin (which protects egg quality), increases cortisol, and impairs FSH and LH regulation. Women who sleep fewer than 6 hours per night have been shown to have lower fertility rates.
sleep.practice: Create a wind-down routine: No screens 30 min before bed, dim lights, use lavender essential oil, and keep your bedroom cool (18–20°C).

exercise.title: Movement & Stress Relief
exercise.content: Regular moderate exercise is one of the most effective stress relievers. It releases endorphins, improves sleep, and helps regulate hormones. However, excessive intense exercise (marathon training, high-intensity daily workouts) can suppress ovulation. Balance is key.
exercise.practice: This week, aim for 30 minutes of moderate movement 5 days: walking, yoga, swimming, or dancing. Notice how your mood shifts afterward.

socialSupport.title: Building Your Support Network
socialSupport.content: Social isolation amplifies stress. Sharing your fertility journey with trusted friends, family, or a support group can significantly reduce emotional burden. You don't have to share with everyone — even one supportive person makes a difference.
socialSupport.practice: Identify one person you trust. Share one thing about your journey with them this week. Connection is healing.

professionalHelp.title: When to Seek Professional Support
professionalHelp.content: If anxiety or sadness about fertility is affecting your daily life, sleep, relationships, or work, professional support can help. Therapists specializing in reproductive mental health understand the unique challenges of this journey. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) is particularly effective for fertility-related anxiety.
professionalHelp.practice: If you're considering therapy, look for a therapist who specializes in reproductive psychology or perinatal mental health. Many offer virtual sessions.

acceptance.title: Practicing Acceptance
acceptance.content: Acceptance doesn't mean giving up — it means acknowledging that some things are beyond your control while focusing on what you can influence. This shift from control to acceptance can significantly reduce stress and improve emotional wellbeing during the fertility journey.
acceptance.practice: Repeat this affirmation: 'I am doing everything I can. I trust my body and my journey. The outcome does not define my worth.'

No reemplaza el consejo, diagnóstico o tratamiento médico profesional. Todos los exámenes y pruebas deben ser discutidos y ordenados por un profesional de la salud calificado.

generalCheckup.title: General Health Checkup
generalCheckup.description: A comprehensive physical exam to assess your overall health, blood pressure, weight, and discuss your pregnancy plans.

bloodWork.title: Complete Blood Count (CBC)
bloodWork.description: Checks for anemia, infections, and overall blood health. Identifies iron deficiency that should be addressed before pregnancy.

pap.title: Pap Smear / Cervical Screening
pap.description: Screens for cervical abnormalities. Best done before pregnancy to address any issues without the added complexity of pregnancy.

thyroid.title: Thyroid Function Test (TSH)
thyroid.description: Thyroid disorders are common and can significantly affect fertility and pregnancy. Both hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism should be managed before conceiving.

rubella.title: Rubella (German Measles) Immunity
rubella.description: Rubella during pregnancy can cause serious birth defects. If you're not immune, vaccination is recommended at least 1 month before trying to conceive.

hepatitis.title: Hepatitis B & C Screening
hepatitis.description: Hepatitis B can be transmitted to the baby during birth. Knowing your status allows for preventive measures. Hepatitis C requires monitoring during pregnancy.

hiv.title: HIV & STI Screening
hiv.description: Routine screening for HIV and sexually transmitted infections protects both your health and your future baby's health. Early detection enables effective treatment.

dental.title: Dental Checkup
dental.description: Gum disease is linked to preterm birth and low birth weight. Hormonal changes during pregnancy can worsen existing dental issues. Complete any necessary dental work before conceiving.

geneticScreening.title: Genetic Carrier Screening
geneticScreening.description: Tests whether you or your partner carry genes for conditions like cystic fibrosis, sickle cell disease, or thalassemia. Results help with informed family planning decisions.

mentalHealth.title: Mental Health Assessment
mentalHealth.description: Discuss any history of depression, anxiety, or other mental health conditions with your healthcare provider. Pre-existing conditions can be managed proactively during pregnancy.

medications.title: Medication Review
medications.description: Review all current medications, supplements, and herbal remedies with your doctor. Some medications are not safe during pregnancy and alternatives should be arranged in advance.

vaccinations.title: Vaccination Review
vaccinations.description: Ensure you're up to date on recommended vaccinations including MMR, varicella, flu, and Tdap. Some vaccines cannot be given during pregnancy, so complete them beforehand.
